summary: Ancient Greek city-state on the east coast of Sicily, founded as a colony of Corinth and repeatedly identified in clues as the home of Archimedes and the most powerful Greek city on the island. Its history is dominated by a long line of tyrants (Gelon, Dionysius I, Agathocles, Hiero II) punctuated by the Corinthian liberator Timoleon, by its central role fending off both Carthage (the Sicilian Wars) and Athens (the disastrous Sicilian Expedition of the Peloponnesian War), and by its final fall to Rome under Marcus Claudius Marcellus in 212 BCE, during which Archimedes was killed.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Clues most often identify Syracuse simply as the home of Archimedes and the most powerful, largest Greek city on Sicily, a Corinthian colony targeted by the Athenians' disastrous Sicilian Expedition. A few isolated later facts round out its identity, including a brief stint as a Byzantine imperial capital.

clues.clue: Identified as the home/birthplace of Archimedes and the largest, most powerful Greek city on the island of Sicily
examples: The birthplace of Archimedes, this city was the largest on an island targeted in a disastrous Athenian expedition during the Peloponnesian War.
For 10 points, name this major Greek city of ancient Sicily that was home to Archimedes.

clues.clue: Founded as a colony of Corinth
examples: For 10 points, name this Corinthian colony, a large city on Sicily.
This other colony of Corinth was later led by the Corinthian Timoleon.

clues.clue: Byzantine emperor Constans II moved his court here after losing support in Constantinople, and was assassinated in this city
examples: Byzantine emperor Constans II was assassinated in this city, which he had moved to after losing support in Constantinople.

works.name: Gelon, Hiero I, and the Deinomenid Dynasty
works.description: The Deinomenid tyrant Gelon seized power in the early 5th century BCE, restoring the political dominance of the landowning gamoroi elite and, in 480 BCE, defeating the Carthaginian king Hamilcar at the Battle of Himera by sending troops disguised as allies from Selinus (Theron of Acragas was his ally in the fight). Gelon reportedly refused to send aid against the Persian invasion of mainland Greece because he was not offered supreme command, and his dynasty is credited with inventing the quinquereme warship. His brother and successor Hiero I continued the family's rule, winning fame as a patron whose chariot-racing victories were praised by the poet Pindar and defeating the Etruscans and Carthaginians at the Battle of Cumae. The Deinomenid line ended when its last tyrant, Thrasybulus, who according to the historian Diodorus relied "only upon the mercenaries," was overthrown in 466 BCE.

clues.clue: Gelon restored the power of the landowning gamaroi elite and defeated the Carthaginian king Hamilcar at the Battle of Himera (480 BCE)
examples: Another ruler of this city, Gelon, restored the power of the landowning gamaroi and won the Battle of Himera against the Carthaginian king Hamilcar.
This city was once led by Hiero II and won the battle of Himera against Hamilcar I.

clues.clue: Gelon won the battle partly by sending troops disguised as allies from the city of Selinus
examples: A Deinomenid leader of this city won a battle partly by sending troops to pose as allies from the city of Selinus; that leader was Gelo.

clues.clue: This city's leader (Gelon) refused to send aid against the Persian invasion because he was not offered supreme command
examples: This city's leader refused to fight the Persians despite having the largest Greek army at the time because its ruler wasn't offered a command position.

clues.clue: The Deinomenid dynasty invented the quinquereme warship
examples: Gelon was a leader of this Greek city, which invented the quinquireme ship.

clues.clue: Tyrant Hiero I was lauded by the poet Pindar for his chariot-racing victories and defeated the Etruscans and Carthaginians at the Battle of Cumae
examples: Name this most powerful of all the Greek colonies. This Sicilian colony's tyrant Hiero I was lauded by Pindar for his chariot racing, and defeated Etruscans and Carthaginians at Cumae.

clues.clue: The Deinomenid dynasty ended when its last tyrant, Thrasybulus, who relied "only upon the mercenaries," was overthrown in 466 BCE
examples: Diodorus wrote that the last Deinomenid tyrant of Syracuse, who had this name, relied "only upon the mercenaries" before being overthrown in 466.
Early holders of this title were from the Deinomenid dynasty, which ended with the overthrow of Thrasybulus.

works.name: Government & Society
works.description: Beyond its tyrants, clues probe Syracuse's social and political structure: an original ruling class of landowning gamoroi was briefly overthrown by the lower-class killichiroi, and citizens could banish rivals through a leaf-based voting system called petalism. After the failure of the Athenian Sicilian Expedition, Diocles instigated a radical democracy and exiled the more moderate Hermocrates, the general who had helped defend the city and had earlier proposed a Pan-Sicilian peace at the Congress of Gela, later called an "ancient Monroe Doctrine."

clues.clue: The lower-class killichiroi expelled the ruling gamoroi elite (during the Expedition of the Ten Thousand era, when Sosis led 300 Syracusan hoplites)
examples: During the Expedition of the Ten Thousand, Sosis led three hundred hoplites from this city, whose lower class, known as the Killichiroi, expelled its ruling Gamoroi.

clues.clue: Citizens could banish rivals through a leaf-based voting system called petalism
examples: Citizens of this non-Athens city could be banished by a system of votes written on leaves, called petalism.

clues.clue: A radical democracy was installed at the instigation of Diocles, who exiled the more moderate Hermocrates
examples: This city saw a radical democracy installed at the instigation of Diocles, who exiled the more moderate Hermocrates.

clues.clue: A man from this city (Hermocrates) proposed a Pan-Sicilian alliance at the Congress of Gela, later called an "ancient Monroe Doctrine"
examples: A man from this city proposed an alliance at the Congress of Gela that has been called an "ancient Monroe Doctrine".

works.name: Dionysius I ("the Elder")
works.description: The most heavily clued of Syracuse's tyrants, Dionysius I rose to power amid the chaos following the sack of Akragas, conquered nearby Rhegium after enslaving its people, and fortified Ortygia as his stronghold before fighting a war with Carthage (his forces destroyed the Carthaginian city of Motya, whose survivors founded Lilybaeum). He built the stone prison Lautumiae, containing a limestone quarry known as his "Ear," where he allegedly imprisoned the poet Philoxenus for mocking his tragedy The Ransom of Hector after it won a prize; he supposedly drank himself to death upon hearing of the play's victory. He clashed repeatedly with Plato, allegedly selling the philosopher into slavery after an argument over virtue and encouraging the Spartan Pollis to assassinate him aboard a galley; he also forced Arete, wife of his exiled former advisor, to marry Timocrates, and patronized the court historian Philistus while exiling his own uncle Dion. So fearful of assassination that he reportedly let only his daughters cut his hair (using red-hot walnut shells), Dionysius was succeeded by his son, Dionysius II, who eventually gave up the throne to Timoleon in exchange for safe passage to Corinth.

clues.clue: Rose to power amid the chaos following the sack of Akragas and conquered nearby Rhegium after enslaving its people
examples: That ruler of this city rose to power during the chaos following the sack of Akragas and conquered the nearby city of Rhegium.
This man set up a fortress on the island of Ortygia after enslaving the people of Rhegium and fighting a war with Carthage, after which he began his rule as tyrant.

clues.clue: Fortified the island of Ortygia as his stronghold; his war with Carthage saw the destruction of Motya, whose survivors founded Lilybaeum
examples: Survivors of this city's destruction of Motya founded Lilybaeum.

clues.clue: Built the stone prison Lautumiae, containing a quarry known as his "Ear," where he tortured prisoners and allegedly locked up the poet Philoxenus
examples: He built a stone prison called the Lautumiae, and allegedly locked up the poet Philoxenus there.
A ruler of this city tortured prisoners in a cave known as his "ear."

clues.clue: Drank himself to death after his tragedy The Ransom of Hector won first prize at a festival, having earlier been mocked for it by Philoxenus
examples: Another ruler of this city apparently drank himself to death after hearing that The Ransom of Hector had won the Lanaean festival.
That may have happened because Philoxenus ridiculed this ruler's play, titled The Ransom of Hector, which won first prize at the Olympics.

clues.clue: Clashed with Plato over an argument about virtue, allegedly selling him into slavery and encouraging the Spartan Pollis to assassinate him aboard a galley
examples: After a ruler of this city got into an argument with Plato over virtue, that ruler encouraged a Spartan named Pollis to assassinate Plato aboard a galley.
According to Diogenes Laertius, he once sold Plato into slavery. An old limestone quarry in the city is called this man's "Ear".

clues.clue: Forced Arete, wife of an exiled former advisor, to marry Timocrates after selling that advisor's estate
examples: After selling a former advisor's estate, one ruler of this city forced Arete, the wife of that former advisor, to marry Timocrates.

clues.clue: Patronized the court historian Philistus, and exiled his own uncle Dion, who formed an army at Zacynthus to overthrow him
examples: That ruler of this city patronized the historian Philistus and conspired to expel his uncle Dion, who then formed an army at Zacynthus to overthrow him.

clues.clue: So afraid of assassins that he allegedly only let his daughters cut his hair, using red-hot walnut shells
examples: According to Valerius Maximus, one holder of this position was allegedly so afraid of assassins that he only had his hair cut by his daughters, who had to burn it with red-hot walnut shells.

clues.clue: Brought a luxuriously extravagant tent to the Olympic Games, which onlookers trashed
examples: According to Diodorus Siculus, when this ruler brought an extremely luxurious tent to the Olympics, people trashed it.

clues.clue: His son and successor, Dionysius II, gave up the throne to the general Timoleon in exchange for safe passage to Corinth
examples: His son and successor accepted the offer of the general Timoleon to give up the throne in return for safe passage to Corinth.

works.name: The Sicilian Expedition
works.description: The Athenians' catastrophic attempt to conquer Syracuse during the Peloponnesian War, promoted by Alcibiades (who was recalled and defected to Sparta after being implicated in the mutilation of statues called hermai just before departure) and led instead by the reluctant Nicias. Syracuse was reinforced by the Spartan general Gylippus, who defeated both Nicias and Demosthenes; a barber who tried to spread word of the impending attack was tortured for disturbing the city. The expedition ended in total disaster: Nicias was captured, and survivors trampled each other trying to reach water at the Assinarus River.

clues.clue: This city was aided/defended by the outsider Gylippus, a Spartan general who defeated both Nicias and Demosthenes
examples: This city was aided by the outsider Gylippus, who beat down both Demosthenes and Nicias.
A notable defense of this city was aided by Gylippus.

clues.clue: Was the destination of an expedition originally meant to be led by Alcibiades, who was recalled to stand trial after the mutilation of the herms
examples: This city, which was once led by Agathocles, was the destination of an action meant to be led by Alcibiades before he was called to stand trial.
Alcibiades was accused of not only profaning the Eleusinian Mysteries, but also mutilating statues of Hermes on the eve of an attack on this city.

clues.clue: The main target of the expedition led by Nicias, which ended in a crushing defeat for Athens
examples: This city, home to two tyrants named Dionysius, was the main target of an expedition led by Nicias which ended in a crushing defeat for Athens during the Peloponnesian War.
Alcibiades promoted a disastrous attempt to take this city during the Peloponnesian War.

clues.clue: A barber who heard about the coming expedition and tried to spread word was tortured for disturbing the city
examples: An account states that when a barber heard about this event and tried to tell others about it, he ended up being tortured for disturbing the city.

clues.clue: At the expedition's end, defeated Athenian troops trampled each other trying to reach water at the Assinarus River, and Nicias was captured
examples: At the very end of this event, a force's members trampled each other trying to get water at the Assinarus River.
Nicias was captured during this event, which was the brainchild of Alcibiades, who had defected to Sparta immediately prior to it taking place.

clues.clue: Nicias made the Athenian forces wait 27 days to sail home because of a lunar eclipse, helping turn the campaign into disaster
examples: Nicias had the Athenian forces on this campaign during the Peloponnesian War wait 27 days to sail home because of a lunar eclipse, thus helping to turn this expedition into the disaster it is remembered as.

works.name: Timoleon's Liberation
works.description: The Corinthian Timoleon, shunned in his home city for helping kill his own brother Timophanes, was sent to Syracuse at its people's request. He deposed the tyrant Dionysius II, defeated the rival strongman Hicetas of Leontini to secure the city, and won the Battle of the Crimissus over a Carthaginian army under Asdrubal despite being outnumbered, aided by a driving hailstorm. He set up a new constitution for the winners and became a celebrated tyrant himself, later paired with the Roman general Lucius Aemilius Paullus in Plutarch's Parallel Lives.

clues.clue: Killed his own brother Timophanes and was shunned in his home city of Corinth before being sent to Syracuse
examples: This Corinthian leader killed his brother Timophanes and deposed Dionysus II, the tyrant of Syracuse.

clues.clue: Deposed Dionysius II and defeated the rival strongman Hicetas of Leontini to secure control of the city
examples: A man shunned in his home city for helping kill his brother gained this title by defeating Hicetas of Leontini; that man was Timoleon.
This man who won the Battle of the Crimissus against Hicetas, the tyrant of Leontini, to secure a successful end to an expedition that he started from Corinth.

clues.clue: Won the Battle of the Crimissus against a Carthaginian army under Asdrubal, aided by a driving hailstorm, and set up a new constitution afterward
examples: This city defeated Asdrubal at the Crimissus under the command of Timoleon, decades after beating Carthage at the battle of Himera.
These two cities fought at the Battle of the Crimissus, where a driving hailstorm caused an unlikely victory for the general Timoleon, who set up a constitution for the winners after the battle.

clues.clue: Was paired with the Roman general Lucius Aemilius Paullus in Plutarch's Parallel Lives
examples: He beat a Carthaginian army at the Battle of the Crimissus and was paired with Lucius Aemilius Paullus in Plutarch's Parallel Lives.

works.name: Agathocles
works.description: Agathocles seized power after overthrowing the government Timoleon had installed, becoming both tyrant of Syracuse and self-proclaimed king of Sicily. He hired the Campanian mercenaries known as the Mamertines, and after a defeat at Himera, launched an unprecedented invasion of the Carthaginian homeland in Africa in 307 BCE, winning two battles at White Tunis despite his troops being decimated by drinking the salty water of the Himera River en route. During the fighting, he concluded a false treaty with the governor Ophellas in order to lure him into attacking Carthage, then murdered him and absorbed his troops. He was eventually defeated by a general of Pyrrhus of Epirus who took the Syracusan throne as Hiero II.

clues.clue: Overthrew the government Timoleon had installed to become tyrant of Syracuse and self-proclaimed king of Sicily
examples: Originally governed by an elite class called the "gamoroi," this city was briefly ruled by Hippocrates of Gela, and it became the center of a modest empire after its ruler Agathocles overthrew Corinth's Timoleon.
This tyrant of Syracuse and king of Sicily tried to expel Carthage from the island in the late fourth century BCE.

clues.clue: Hired the Campanian mercenaries known as the Mamertines
examples: Another holder of this post hired a band of mercenaries called the Mamertines.

clues.clue: After a defeat at Himera, invaded the Carthaginian homeland in Africa in 307 BCE, winning two battles at White Tunis despite his troops being decimated by drinking the salty water of the Himera River
examples: In a war between these two cities, one side's troops were decimated by drinking the salty water of the Himera River; despite that loss, the tyrant Agathocles led a successful invasion including two battles at White Tunis.
After defeat at Himera, he led the first attack of Carthage on the African continent in 307 BCE.

clues.clue: Concluded a false treaty with the governor Ophellas to lure him into attacking Carthage, then murdered him and absorbed his troops
examples: The leader of one of these cities concluded a false treaty with the governor Ophellas to get him to attack the other, then murdered him and absorbed his troops.

clues.clue: Was eventually defeated by a general of Pyrrhus of Epirus who took the throne under the name Hiero II
examples: That man was Agathocles, who was defeated by a general of Pyrrhus who took this post under the name Hiero II.

works.name: Hiero II
works.description: A former general of Pyrrhus of Epirus, Hiero II saved Syracuse from a siege alongside Pyrrhus before taking the throne himself and renewing conflicts with Carthage, ultimately joining Rome during the First Punic War (which was sparked when his troops defeated the Mamertine mercenaries). He minted coinage bearing the image of his wife Philistis, whom he married to gain aristocratic support after Pyrrhus departed Sicily, and had earlier been appointed strategos by the oligarchic party on the strength of his reputation fighting the Bruttians. Famously, he suspected a goldsmith of stealing gold meant for a votive crown and had a mathematician determine whether it was pure; he also commissioned an enormous grain ship with gardens grown on its decks, possibly the largest ship in the ancient world. His death in 215 BCE ended a long era of Sicilian loyalty to Rome; he was succeeded by his teenaged grandson Hieronymus, who broke the alliance with Rome after the Battle of Cannae and ruled less than a year before being assassinated.

clues.clue: A former general of Pyrrhus of Epirus, who saved this city from a siege before Hiero II took over and renewed conflicts with Carthage
examples: Pyrrhus of Epirus saved this city from a siege; his general Hiero II then took it over and renewed its conflicts with Carthage.

clues.clue: Joined Rome during the First Punic War, which was sparked when his troops defeated the Mamertine mercenaries
examples: Under the teenaged tyrant Hieronymus, this city broke its alliance with Rome after the battle of Cannae, after previously joining Rome during the First Punic War under Hiero II.
A Corinthian named Timoleon became a ruler of this city that sparked the First Punic War by defeating the Mamertines.

clues.clue: Minted coinage bearing the image of his wife Philistis, whom he married to gain aristocratic support after Pyrrhus departed Sicily
examples: One holder of this position minted coinage with the image of their wife Philisitis, who they had married in order to gain aristocratic support following the departure of Pyrrhus from their homeland.

clues.clue: Was first appointed strategos by the oligarchic party after making his reputation fighting the Bruttians
examples: Another holder of this position was first appointed strategos by the oligarchic party, after he had made his reputation fighting the Bruttians.

clues.clue: Suspected a goldsmith of stealing gold meant for a votive crown and had a mathematician (Archimedes) test whether it was pure
examples: A king of this city suspected that a goldsmith was stealing gold he had received to make a votive crown.
This tyrant, a former general of Pyrrhus, supposedly asked a mathematician to determine whether his crown was made of pure gold.

clues.clue: Commissioned an enormous grain ship with gardens grown on its decks, possibly the largest ship in the ancient world
examples: Gardens were grown on the decks of a ship commissioned by this king which may have been the largest in the ancient world.

clues.clue: His death in 215 BCE ended a long era of Sicilian loyalty to Rome; his successor, the teenaged Hieronymus, ruled less than a year before being assassinated
examples: A long era of Sicilian loyalty to Rome ended with the death in 215 B.C.E. of this long-time tyrant of Syracuse, who was succeeded by a son who ruled for less than a year before being assassinated.

works.name: Wars with Carthage (the Sicilian Wars)
works.description: Beyond the campaigns of its individual tyrants, Syracuse fought a centuries-long series of conflicts with Carthage over control of Sicily, sometimes called the Sicilian Wars. During one such conflict, Euryalus Castle was defended from a Carthaginian cavalry assault led by the exiled defector Deinocrates, and a civil war between Thoenon and Sostratus forced Syracuse to call in the foreign king Pyrrhus of Epirus, whose wife Lanassa had ties to the city, for aid against Carthage.

clues.clue: Euryalus Castle was defended from a Carthaginian cavalry assault led by the exiled defector Deinocrates
examples: During a conflict between these two cities, Euryalus Castle was defended from a cavalry assault led by the exiled defector Deinocrates.

clues.clue: A civil war between Thoenon and Sostratus forced this city to call in the foreign king Pyrrhus of Epirus, whose wife Lanassa had ties to the city
examples: A civil war between Thoenon and Sostratus led one of these cities to be invaded by the other, forcing it to call in a foreign ruler whose wife Lanassa had ties to the city.

clues.clue: The conflicts with Carthage were fought across generations by rulers such as Dionysius I and the Carthaginian commander Hamilcar the Magonid
examples: For 10 points, name these ancient cities led during the Sicilian Wars by people like Dionysius I and Hamilcar the Magonid.

works.name: The Syracusan Navy
works.description: Syracuse's naval forces conducted operations across the central Mediterranean: they lifted a siege of a Greek colony on the island of Hvar by defeating the Illyrian Liburnians at the Battle of Pharos, used animal hides to deflect enemy grappling hooks in battle, and combined with forces from Cumae (the first Greek colony on the Italian mainland) to decisively defeat the Etruscans in the Bay of Naples. Their conflict with Carthage brought about the first use of quinqueremes by the fleet, though the Carthaginian commander Mago once crushed the Syracusan navy at the Battle of Catana, leading to an unsuccessful siege of the city.

clues.clue: Lifted a siege of a Greek colony on the island of Hvar by defeating the Illyrian Liburnians at the Battle of Pharos
examples: This force lifted a siege of a Greek colony on the island of Hvar by defeating the Illyrian Liburnians at the Battle of Pharos.

clues.clue: Used animal hides to deflect enemy grappling hooks in battle
examples: During one engagement, this force used animal hides in order to deflect enemy grappling hooks.

clues.clue: Was crushed by the Carthaginian commander Mago at the Battle of Catana, leading to an unsuccessful siege of Syracuse
examples: The Carthaginian commander Mago crushed this force at the Battle of Catana, leading to an unsuccessful siege of the city this force was based in.

clues.clue: Combined with forces from Cumae, the first Greek colony on the Italian mainland, to defeat the Etruscans in the Bay of Naples
examples: In one battle, this force combined with forces from the first Greek colony on the Italian mainland to decisively defeat the Etruscans in the Bay of Naples.

clues.clue: Was victorious at the Battle of Cumae and helped defeat the Athenian expedition led by Nicias
examples: This military force was victorious at the Battle of Cumae and assisted in the defeat of an expedition led by Nicias.

works.name: Roman Conquest & the Death of Archimedes
works.description: After the teenaged tyrant Hieronymus broke Syracuse's alliance with Rome following the Battle of Cannae, the city allied with Carthage under the magistrates Hippocrates and Epicydes and was besieged by the consul Marcus Claudius Marcellus during the Second Punic War beginning in 214 BCE. The city's celebration of a festival for Artemis allowed Marcellus's forces to infiltrate it — an opening created by the traitor Moeriscus, whose betrayal also enabled the first use of Heracleides of Tarentum's siege engine, the sambuca. Before its fall, Archimedes was hired to design defense mechanisms including a giant claw to shake enemy ships and a heat ray made of parabolic mirrors. When the city finally fell in 212 BCE, Archimedes was killed by a Roman soldier despite his legendary last words, "do not disturb my circles."

clues.clue: The teenaged tyrant Hieronymus broke this city's alliance with Rome after Cannae; the city then allied with Carthage under magistrates Hippocrates and Epicydes
examples: During the Second Punic War, Marcellus commanded troops that besieged this city, which had declared for Carthage under the leadership of the magistrates Hippocrates and Epicydes.

clues.clue: Besieged by the consul Marcus Claudius Marcellus beginning in 214 BCE during the Second Punic War
examples: Gylippus was enlisted to defend this city, which was besieged by Marcus Claudius Marcellus in 214 BCE.
Marcus Claudius Marcellus led one of these campaigns during the Second Punic War during which a scientist legendarily deployed a heat ray.

clues.clue: A festival for Artemis allowed Marcellus's forces to infiltrate the city, aided by the traitor Moeriscus, whose betrayal enabled the first use of Heracleides of Tarentum's siege engine, the sambuca
examples: This city's celebration of a festival for Artemis allowed Romans under Marcus Claudius Marcellus to infiltrate and capture it.
One of these campaigns only succeeded because Moeriscus betrayed his city which was celebrating a festival of Artemis; that campaign saw the first use of Heracleides of Tarentum's new sambuca.

clues.clue: Archimedes was hired to build a giant claw to shake enemy ships and a heat ray made of parabolic mirrors to defend the city
examples: While defending this city from a Roman siege, this city's ruler Hieron II supposedly hired an inventor from this city to build defense mechanisms like a claw and a heat ray.
This city held off a Roman siege with a collection of mirrors that formed a parabolic reflector.

clues.clue: When the city fell in 212 BCE, Archimedes was killed by a Roman soldier despite his last words, "do not disturb my circles"
examples: Legendarily a man died in this city after telling a soldier "do not disturb my circles", despite the orders of Marcus Claudius Marcellus.
Archimedes was killed when Roman troops finally captured it in 212 B.C.E.

clues.clue: Epicydes, the last tyrant of Syracuse, allegedly tried to defend the city using a giant ship-burning mirror
examples: The last holder of this office, Epicydes, allegedly attempted to defend his city from the Romans using a giant ship-burning mirror.

comprehensive_summary: Syracuse was a Greek city on the east coast of Sicily, founded as a colony of Corinth, and clues most often identify it simply as the home of Archimedes and the most powerful Greek city on the island. In the early 5th century BCE, the Deinomenid tyrant Gelon restored the power of the landowning gamoroi elite and defeated the Carthaginian king Hamilcar at the Battle of Himera (480 BCE) by disguising troops as allies from Selinus; his brother Hiero I, praised by Pindar for chariot racing, continued the family's rule until the dynasty's last tyrant, Thrasybulus, was overthrown in 466 BCE. The most heavily clued tyrant, Dionysius I ("the Elder"), rose after the sack of Akragas, conquered Rhegium, imprisoned the poet Philoxenus in his "Ear" prison-quarry after a play of his own composition won a festival prize, and repeatedly clashed with Plato. Syracuse's central role in the Peloponnesian War came via the Sicilian Expedition, the disastrous Athenian invasion promoted by Alcibiades (recalled after the mutilation of the herms) and led by a reluctant Nicias; Syracuse was saved by the Spartan Gylippus, and the invaders were annihilated at the Assinarus River. After the Expedition, Diocles installed a radical democracy and exiled the general Hermocrates, who had proposed a Pan-Sicilian peace at the Congress of Gela. The Corinthian general Timoleon later deposed the tyrant Dionysius II and won the Battle of the Crimissus over Carthage; he was succeeded in turn by Agathocles, who hired the Mamertine mercenaries and launched an invasion of Carthage's African homeland in 307 BCE after betraying the governor Ophellas. Agathocles was eventually defeated by Hiero II, a former general of Pyrrhus of Epirus, who allied with Rome in the First Punic War, married Philistis, and — in a story that also involves Archimedes — suspected a goldsmith of adulterating a votive crown's gold. Syracuse's navy fought across the Mediterranean, from the Battle of Pharos off Hvar to the Battle of Cumae against the Etruscans. The city's alliance with Rome collapsed under Hiero II's teenaged successor Hieronymus after the Battle of Cannae, leading to a Roman siege under Marcus Claudius Marcellus beginning in 214 BCE; a festival of Artemis allowed the traitor Moeriscus to let Roman forces infiltrate, and despite Archimedes' defensive claw and heat ray, the city fell in 212 BCE, with Archimedes killed by a Roman soldier despite his plea not to disturb his circles. Centuries later, the Byzantine emperor Constans II briefly moved his court to Syracuse before being assassinated there.
recursive_suggestions: Dionysius I of Syracuse — the most densely-clued single ruler here (dedicated tossup + multiple bonus parts); could support a standalone figure page
Timoleon — dedicated bonus-part content on his Sicilian liberation campaign; potential standalone figure page
Agathocles — dedicated bonus-part content on his African invasion; potential standalone figure page
Hiero II — dedicated bonus-part content; potential standalone figure page
Sicilian Expedition — appeared as its own answerline across a tossup and two bonus parts with dense, independent content; strong standalone candidate

Archimedes of Syracuse — appeared as its own tossup answerline with Science-category clues (Sand Reckoner, cattle problem, 14-piece puzzle); belongs on a separate Science page, not folded into this History page
Sicily — appeared repeatedly as a broader-region answerline (Byzantine/Arab/Norman period content); potential standalone Geography/History topic
Magna Graecia — appeared as its own answerline covering the wider network of Greek colonies in southern Italy
Aghlabid conquest of Sicily — appeared as its own 9th-century answerline; potential standalone topic
Ambiguity flag: the "Collatz conjecture" surfaced repeatedly because it is nicknamed the "Syracuse problem/function" — unrelated Math topic, excluded entirely

Ambiguity flag: a bonus on 1937 American union-busting (Ford, Mohawk Valley formula, La Follette Committee) surfaced because the "Mohawk Valley formula" prompts on "Syracuse" (the New York city) — unrelated modern topic, excluded entirely
Ambiguity flag: Theocritus of Syracuse surfaced as a Literature answerline (bucolic poet); excluded from this History page
